item.title: Seruvakula: Fiji can host future fixtures
item.description: FIJI Water Flying Fijians head coach Senirusi Seruvakula hopes Fiji will be in a position to host Nations Championship matches within the next three years, saying improved stadium infrastructure is vital for Fiji’s rugby future. Following Fiji’s 33-17 loss to Scotland, Seruvakula said the passionate support from Fijian fans in Edinburgh highlighted what international rugby could look like if top-t
item.content:encoded:
FIJI Water Flying Fijians head coach Senirusi Seruvakula hopes Fiji will be in a position to host Nations Championship matches within the next three years, saying improved stadium infrastructure is vital for Fiji’s rugby future.

Following Fiji’s 33-17 loss to Scotland, Seruvakula said the passionate support from Fijian fans in Edinburgh highlighted what international rugby could look like if top-tier nations were able to travel to Fiji.

“I think this is the best,” Seruvakula said when asked about the atmosphere during the tour.

“A lot of Fijians turned up today (Sunday), the weather was good and hopefully one day we’ll host a game.”

At present, Fiji cannot stage Nations Championship fixtures because it does not have a stadium that meets the tournament’s minimum seating requirements.

“We don’t have the capacity of the stadium back home,” Seruvakula said.

“But this is good for a start so that we can get a lot of revenue and then build our stadium.”

He hopes investment into infrastructure will eventually allow Fiji to welcome Northern Hemisphere nations for major Test matches.

“For the next three years, hopefully the people leading Fiji Rugby will build a stadium so the teams from the Northern Hemisphere can travel to Fiji.”

Seruvakula said he had been informed Fiji would require a stadium with a capacity of about 30,000 spectators to qualify as a host venue.

He confirmed that even the HFC Bank Stadium in Suva would not meet those requirements.

As a result, Fiji is unlikely to host Pacific Nations Cup matches in the immediate future.

“I think in three years’ time,” Seruvakula said.

Despite the Scotland defeat, Seruvakula said the campaign has provided valuable lessons for the coaching staff.

“There are a lot of learnings from these three games.

“We now know how we can improve as a team and also in selecting the players. We know where they are at the moment, who we need to bring in and how we monitor them. It’s a lot of learning.”

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: From regret to rugby inspiration
item.description: WHEN Aradhna Lal walks onto the rugby field, she brings more than just a whistle and a game plan. She brings resilience, a willingness to learn and a determination to prove that passion, not background defines a coach. Originally from Labasa, Lal is the head of department for Home Economics at Batinikama College, where she has been based since 2016.
item.content:encoded:
WHEN Aradhna Lal walks onto the rugby field, she brings more than just a whistle and a game plan. She brings resilience, a willingness to learn and a determination to prove that passion, not background defines a coach.

Originally from Labasa, Lal is the head of department for Home Economics at Batinikama College, where she has been based since 2016.

While many know her today as a member of the coaching staff for the school’s under-17 Deans rugby side, her sporting journey began long before she stepped onto a rugby field.

When she was a student of Labasa Primary School, she represented her school in netball before discovering a passion for athletics at Labasa College. Specialising in the 800m, she progressed through the Macuata zone competitions and earned selection to represent her school at the prestigious Coca-Cola Games.

Yet, instead of competing, fear got the better of her.

Afraid of travelling to Suva and performing in front of a large crowd, she withdrew from the competition by producing a medical certificate which stated she had asthma.

Looking back, Lal describes that decision as one of her biggest regrets in life.

“Looking back, I realise fear had robbed me of a once-in-a-lifetime opportunity. I would have been the only Indo-Fijian female athlete in that event, and my participation would have created history, regardless of result,” Lal said

Lal said the experience has shaped the way she now mentors young athletes.

“I always tell them to never let fear stop them from chasing opportunities. You may fail but you should never regret trying again.”

Although football was the sport many associated her with, Lal had always enjoyed watching rugby, particularly the Fiji sevens team. Her journey into coaching began last year when Batinikama College introduced its U-17 boys rugby program.

Encouraged by the school’s PEMAC head of department, Mr Navunisino, she embraced the challenge despite having no experience in rugby.

She was holding a senior football coaching licence by then so Lal immersed herself in learning rugby through coaching courses and practical experience.

“I wanted to support our students while also expanding my own knowledge of the sport. The more I learned, the more passionate I became.”

Communication proved to be the utmost barrier because many of her players understood rugby concepts better in the iTaukei language. Limited training resources also forced the coaching staff to think creatively, using coconut trunks as weights and large vehicle tyres for strength and conditioning.

Rather than viewing those obstacles as setback, Lal saw them as opportunities to innovate.

Her efforts, alongside fellow coach Irene Goundar and the school’s coaching staff, helped guide Batinikama College to the zone title, a milestone she describes as one of her proudest moments of her coaching career.

Another memorable achievement came during her World Rugby Level Two coaching assessment when she chose to conduct a line-out session, one of the more technically demanding areas of the game.

Her assessor questioned her decision, noting that most coaches preferred simpler sessions. Lal trusted her preparation, successfully delivered the session and earned praise from the assessor.

“That experience taught me an important lesson, growth happens when we are willing to step outside our comfort zone.”

Today, Lal is continuing her coaching education while serving as the manager of the Macuata women’s rugby team under a three-year contract, where she also assists with coaching duties.

Her long-term ambition is to coach at national level, but beyond personal success, she hopes to inspire more women to enter rugby coaching.

“I entered rugby without ever playing the sport, or having family background in rugby. If I can do it, then so can any woman who is willing to learn, work and believe in herself.”

For Lal, rugby is no longer simply a sport.

It is a platform to inspire young people, challenge stereotypes and remind others that the biggest barriers are often the ones we place on ourselves.

Batinikama College’s under-17 Deans rugby coaching official and home economics teacher, Aradhna Lal. Picture: SUPPLIED

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Ba edges Navua on home turf
item.description: DAYALS Sawmillers Ba FC defeated Prithvi Navua FC 1-0 after a thrilling and tough round 14 match of the Extra Premier League at the Four R Stadium, Govind Park on Sunday. Ba head coach Ravinesh Kumar credited a change in tactics and the unwavering support of the home crowd.
item.content:encoded:
DAYALS Sawmillers Ba FC defeated Prithvi Navua FC 1-0 after a thrilling and tough round 14 match of the Extra Premier League at the Four R Stadium, Govind Park on Sunday.

Ba head coach Ravinesh Kumar credited a change in tactics and the unwavering support of the home crowd.

The victory completed another successful outing against Navua, with Ba backing up their 3-1 win over the same opponents in the 2026 BIC Fiji FACT final in Labasa.

The clash on Sunday carried the feel of a rematch, with both teams producing an entertaining contest in front of a large crowd.

Kumar acknowledged Navua’s quality and said his players had to work hard for all three points.

“I believe this was a very good game and a very tough game as well,” Kumar said.

“Navua gave us a very good run. We know they are a good team because we played them in the Fiji FACT final and they played really well.”

After a nil-all at first half, Kumar said the key message during the break was to abandon attempts to attack through the congested middle and instead use the width of the field.

“We were trying to penetrate through the middle, but their defence was compact inside, so I told the boys to get out to the flanks and play from there. The process came from the flanks, and we scored through the flanks, so that was the good part,” he said.

The tactical adjustment paid off as Ba found the breakthrough in the second spell to secure a valuable victory and keep their momentum going in the league.

“The first thing is recovery and then we’ll plan for the Lautoka game,” he said.

“There is always pressure when we play at home. I don’t feel good when our fans go home with their heads down, so today I’m happy that they are going home happy.”

Kumar also reserved special praise for the supporters who packed Govind Park and urged them to continue backing the Men in Black as they chase further success this season.

Ba will meet Lautoka this Sunday at Govind Park.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Bout with plot twist
item.description: A TWIST to the main bout of this Saturday’s Kings Boxing Promotion event in Suva sees Mikaele Ravalaca facing a new opponent, after Rohit Chauhan of India pulled out from the fight. Ravalaca will now fight India’s Digari Mahesh for the WBA Oceania Lightweight title, which was confirmed by promoter Nahroon Buksh in a press conference yesterday.
item.content:encoded:
A TWIST to the main bout of this Saturday’s Kings Boxing Promotion event in Suva sees Mikaele Ravalaca facing a new opponent, after Rohit Chauhan of India pulled out from the fight.

Ravalaca will now fight India’s Digari Mahesh for the WBA Oceania Lightweight title, which was confirmed by promoter Nahroon Buksh in a press conference yesterday.

Buksh emphasised the promotion believed in providing the best for boxing fans.

“First of all, King’s Boxing Promotion believes in giving the best to the boxing fans,” Buksh said.

The promoter explained Chauhan’s airfares were paid, contract signed and also produced a medical certificate.

“There’s nothing from our end; we are all clear. We have done our work on time and as required,” he added.

This was after Boxing Commission of Fiji chairman, Adi Narayan, explained Chauhan “backed out” from the bout in the last minute.

“Reason that was given to us is not satisfactory; we are very disappointed after the promoter (Buksh) has paid the airfares, and not only for him (but) his manager, plus another person to come with him.

Narayan said the matter would be taken up to the Indian Boxing Council and the World Boxing Association.

Meanwhile, Ravalaca, also present at the conference, told reporters he had been studying his new opponent closely.

“I’ve been studying him and I know he’s training well. He was supposed to fight last week in Australia and I know he’s in camp,” the title holder from Ra said.

“I know he’s in camp and I don’t underestimate him because he knows I’m already training and he will be ready for this fight.”

This Saturday, the gates of the Vodafone Arena will open at 1pm.

The promotion begins from 3pm, with four exhibition fights and 15 professional bouts confirmed.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: The ‘Ba toh Ba hai’ finale
item.description: THE soccer crazy town of Ba flips the script once more this Saturday when the McDonald’s Fiji Drua Women and the NSW Waratahs Women will meet there for the grand final of the Super W competition. It will be a reunion of foes for the two sides; the Drua Women were thrashed 29-42 in Sydney, Australia in round one of the competition.
item.content:encoded:
THE soccer crazy town of Ba flips the script once more this Saturday when the McDonald’s Fiji Drua Women and the NSW Waratahs Women will meet there for the grand final of the Super W competition.

It will be a reunion of foes for the two sides; the Drua Women were thrashed 29-42 in Sydney, Australia in round one of the competition.

The loss was head coach Mike Legge’s first result with the team, and since then, the team has built up its stride and strength to topple opponents throughout the competition.

It did so with great fanfare last Saturday at Churchill Park in Lautoka when they beat the Queensland Reds side 31-5 in front of a large turnout of fans, family members and a community of supporters who came out to see the women succeed.

Now, the team heads down to Ba to meet the Waratahs there.

The Waratahs reach our shores this week with a commanding force of attention, after they won 33-22 over the Western Force in Perth last week.

And making the four hour flight from Sydney to Nadi in the coming days means the side will increase the tempo in their engine room of defenders to ensure they earn a back-to-back win of the Super W competition when the dust settles in Ba this Saturday.

The need to win is of the essence for the Sydney warriors; they need a back-to-back win and to add a sixth Super W trophy to their cabinet.

However, as the quest for greatness reaches a fever pitch this week, the winner of their clash will ascend to the next level to meet the winner of the Super Rugby Aupiki competition, the New Zealand segment of the women’s competition, on August 1.

The Drua Women compete in the Australian segment.

Kick-off this Saturday will be at 1.05pm.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Nasova stays in Fiji as Newcastle move called off
item.description: Fijian star Joji Nasova will not be joining Newcastle next season after the Red Bulls agreed to release him from his contract. The 25-year-old wing, who won a sevens silver medal at the Paris Olympics, had signed to move to the ambitious Prem club but will now stay with Fijian Drua.
item.content:encoded:
Fijian star Joji Nasova will not be joining Newcastle next season after the Red Bulls agreed to release him from his contract.

The 25-year-old wing, who won a sevens silver medal at the Paris Olympics, had signed to move to the ambitious Prem club but will now stay with Fijian Drua.

A club statement put the collapse of the deal down to “personal reasons”.

“While we were very much looking forward to welcoming Joji to Newcastle Red Bulls, we fully understand and respect the unique personal circumstances that have led to this decision,” Red Bulls general manager Neil McIlroy told the club wesbite. , external

“Contractual certainty is vital for clubs, players and the wider game. But at the same time rugby is built on people, families and communities, and we believe our responsibility as an employer extends beyond the field.

“We wish Joji, his family and the Fijian Drua every success for the future.”

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Curbing yellow and red cards pays off for MGM ruggers
item.description: MENDING weaknesses in their level of discipline paid off for the Mahatma Gandhi Memorial High School under-16 Deans rugby squad, as they defeated Suva Grammar School 18-11 in the Southern Zone semi-finals last Saturday. This was pointed out by team captain Waisiki Ramatakubou, who shared the team began the week with an intent of minimising the number of yellow and red cards shown, which paid off h
item.content:encoded:
MENDING weaknesses in their level of discipline paid off for the Mahatma Gandhi Memorial High School under-16 Deans rugby squad, as they defeated Suva Grammar School 18-11 in the Southern Zone semi-finals last Saturday.

This was pointed out by team captain Waisiki Ramatakubou, who shared the team began the week with an intent of minimising the number of yellow and red cards shown, which paid off handsomely in the end.

“We focused on our discipline in light of how we performed last week; there were many disciplinary cases (issues) noted such as two red cards shown,” Ramatakubou said.

“It was a reckless thing to show on our part.”

However, he said his teammates were pleased no cards were shown to them in their semi-final clash.

“But today (Saturday), we were happy that no red or yellow cards were given out to us today (Saturday).

“Other areas of our performance such as our defence and our attack were carried out very well.”

Grammar were stunned by the MGM defence and the set-pieces executed by the young ruggers of Vatuwaqa, Suva, as they broke through the tough Lions defence to make their mark felt.

The MGM U16 squad this year is made up of last year’s U15 squad, which lost 27-24 to Grammar in the 2025 Vodafone Deans national finals, held at the HFC Bank Stadium in Suva.

Saturday’s win saw them avenge themselves for last year’s loss, where they settled as runners-up.

In light of this, Ramatakubou said the team was thankful for the win earned and set their sights on the final this Saturday against Nasinu Secondary School.

The victory over the young lions of Veiuto means MGM books a spot in the national quarter-finals of the 2026 Vodafone Deans competition.

Meanwhile, Nasinu had beaten Marist Brothers High School 16-15 to meet MGM this week.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.

item.title: Naisewa’s double feat
item.description: McDonald’s Fiji Drua Women loosehead prop Karalaini Naisewa celebrated one of the biggest moments of her rugby career reaching her 30th cap in style as the Drua defeated the Queensland Reds Women 31-5 in their Super Rugby Women’s home semi-final at Churchill Park in Lautoka yesterday. The experienced front-rower described the occasion as a blessing, admitting she never imagined she would one day a
item.content:encoded:
McDonald’s Fiji Drua Women loosehead prop Karalaini Naisewa celebrated one of the biggest moments of her rugby career reaching her 30th cap in style as the Drua defeated the Queensland Reds Women 31-5 in their Super Rugby Women’s home semi-final at Churchill Park in Lautoka yesterday.

The experienced front-rower described the occasion as a blessing, admitting she never imagined she would one day achieve the milestone in the Drua jersey.

“It’s very special. I wasn’t expecting to reach these 30 caps, but I want to thank God for giving me this talent,” Naisewa said.

“Without Him I couldn’t make it this far. I also thank the girls, my family and the management for trusting me to do the work.”

The occasion became even more memorable when Naisewa scored the second try in the first half with a five-pointer.

She said running onto the field, her only focus was ensuring her 30th appearance would end in a win.

“It just came to my mind that my 30 caps needed to be special to me,” she said.

“Without the girls it wouldn’t be special. Thank you to the girls for this moment. It was a very special 30 caps for me and scoring that try made it even more special.”

Despite the convincing performance, Naisewa believes there is still work to be done before the home grand final.

She identified defence, tackle execution and set-piece accuracy as areas the team must improve if they are to lift the title next weekend.

“I think it’s just our defence going into the final and some of our tackling and set piece, so we’ll brush off the semi-final, learn from our mistakes this week and make it a good final next week,” she said.

Naisewa dedicated both the victory and her personal milestone to her family, thanking them for their unwavering support throughout her rugby journey.

She also acknowledged the passionate Drua supporters who packed Churchill Park, saying their energy played a significant role in the team’s success.

“I dedicate this win to my family for always supporting me and to the fans for coming out in numbers,” she said.

“Without them we couldn’t make it this far. Next week, come out in numbers again and support the girls.”

Naisewa will be hoping her memorable 30th cap becomes the perfect steppingstone towards adding a Super Rugby Women’s title to an already unforgettable week.

© 2026 FijiTimes. All rights reserved.
